channel.title: Basics of Data Management
channel.description: Recent content on Basics of Data Management

item.title: Data Validation
item.description:
This video covers how to use data validation to control the type of data that can be entered into a cell, including drop-down lists and custom error messages.

This video provides a practical guide on using Data Validation to maintain data integrity in Excel. The instructor demonstrates how to limit user input to specific values, ensuring accuracy and reducing entry errors.

item.title: Introduction to Data Visualization
item.description:
This video introduces the concept of data visualization, why it is important, and the principles of effective data visualization.

This video serves as an introduction to data visualization (0:02 - 0:28), explaining how it transforms raw numbers and statistics into meaningful visual stories (0:30 - 1:05).

item.title: Introduction to Google Sheets
item.description:
This page introduces the interface and features of Google Sheets, including creating new spreadsheets and importing data from different sources.

This video serves as an introductory guide to Google Sheets for data analysis, part of the Data Management Training course. It highlights the similarities between Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel (noting that they are 99% identical) while emphasizing the unique advantages of using Google Sheets, such as its cloud-based architecture that enables real-time collaboration (1:08-1:43).

item.title: Module 4 - 1 - Introduction to Google Forms (Part 1)
item.description:
This is the first video in Module 4 - Data Collection with Google Forms. It provides an overview of what Google Forms is and covers the basic aspects of creating a new form.

This video serves as a comprehensive guide for beginners on how to start using Google Forms for data collection.

item.title: The Basics
item.description:
This video provides an overview of what Excel is, what it can be used for, what the interface looks like, and the basic terminology related to spreadsheets.

This video provides a foundational overview of Microsoft Excel for beginners. It covers the essential components of the Excel interface, including:

item.title: Conditional Formatting
item.description:
This video shows how to use conditional formatting to highlight important data, including colour scales and data bars.

This video provides a tutorial on using conditional formatting to analyze and visualize datasets (0:00-12:00). The video demonstrates how to:

  • Apply Custom Rules: Using formulas to compare columns, such as identifying schools with more girls than boys (1:40-7:07).
  • Use Fixed vs. Variable References: Explaining how the use of dollar signs ($) controls cell locking when applying formatting across rows (3:47-6:07).
  • Highlight Specific Data: Setting rules to flag values based on criteria, such as schools with more than 35 boys (7:11-7:48) or those with zero teachers (7:50-8:48).
  • Incorporate Logic: Using formulas like SUM within conditional formatting to identify schools with specific social category enrollment (8:51-10:07).

The video emphasizes that conditional formatting is dynamic, meaning the highlights automatically update whenever the underlying data changes (10:35-11:07).

item.title: Creating a Basic Spreadsheet
item.description:
This video shows how to create a new spreadsheet, enter and format data, adjust column widths and row heights, and use basic formatting options like bold and italic.

Key topics include:

  • Creating and Populating Data: Setting up a new spreadsheet and entering data into columns and rows (0:30 - 1:49).
  • Basic Formatting: Enhancing data presentation using tools like Bold and Italic to differentiate headings from body text, as well as applying custom text and background colors (1:53 - 3:33).
  • Managing Cell Sizing: Techniques to adjust column widths and row heights to ensure content is fully visible. This includes manual dragging and the efficient double-click shortcut for automatic resizing (3:35 - 6:05).
  • Text Handling: Using the Wrap Text feature to manage longer entries within a single cell (6:06 - 8:06).

By the end of the video, viewers will have a functional understanding of how to organize, format, and size a spreadsheet for readability.

item.title: Good Practices for Data Visualization
item.description:
This video covers chart types, choosing the right chart, simplifying charts, using labels and titles well, selecting colour schemes, and avoiding common mistakes such as 3D charts.

This video provides an overview of good practices for creating three common chart types to ensure they are effective and easy to interpret:

item.title: Module 4 - 2 - Introduction to Google Forms (Part 2)
item.description:
This is the second part of the first video in Module 4 - Data Collection with Google Forms. It shows how the form preview can be used when creating a new Google Form.

This video focuses on advanced features for efficient data collection. It covers how to apply response validation (such as ensuring phone numbers are 10 digits or validating email formats), import questions from previous forms to save time (1:46), and organize long surveys by using sections (2:50).

item.title: Module 5 - 2 - Formulas and Pivot Tables in Google Sheets
item.description:
This is the second video in Module 5 - Data Analysis with Google Sheets. It shows how to use formulas and pivot tables in Google Sheets and points out some interface differences compared with Microsoft Excel.

This video serves as a practical guide for users transitioning from Microsoft Excel to Google Sheets. It demonstrates how the two platforms share similar core functionalities while highlighting specific advantages of Google Sheets.

item.title: IF Statements
item.description:
This video provides a comprehensive tutorial on using various IF statements to analyze data and define cell outcomes. Key topics covered include:

  • Basic IF Statements: Using IF to create conditional logic (e.g., checking if girls’ enrollment is greater than boys’) (1:22 - 3:51).
  • Multiple Conditions with AND: Combining the AND logical operator with IF to test for multiple criteria simultaneously (4:13 - 7:52).
  • Counting and Summing with Criteria: Utilizing COUNTIF and SUMIF for single-condition data analysis (7:55 - 10:41).
  • Advanced Aggregation: Using COUNTIFS and SUMIFS to handle multiple criteria for counting or summing data ranges (10:42 - 13:20).

The instructor emphasizes the utility of these functions for data management and suggests practicing with the provided exercises to master these concepts (15:03 - 15:34).

item.title: Module 4 - 3 - Adding Images and Videos
item.description:
This video provides a practical guide on how to enhance Google Forms by integrating images and videos (0:02-0:15).

Key highlights include:

  • Adding Media Elements: Demonstrates how to insert videos directly from YouTube (0:36-0:49) or upload images from your computer or other sources (0:26-0:32).
  • Section Management: Explains how to use these media elements to break up a form into smaller, organized sections, including adding custom titles and descriptions (1:51-2:21).
  • Interactive Questions: Shows how to attach images directly to specific questions, allowing for more engaging data collection (2:46-3:50).

The tutorial concludes by noting that these features are particularly useful for creating larger, more complex forms, and previews upcoming lessons on managing responses and sharing settings (4:10-4:28).

item.title: Module 5 - 3 - Collaborating on Google Sheets
item.description:
This video is the final installment in the Module 5: Data Analysis with Google Sheets series.

The video focuses on sharing and collaboration features. It guides viewers through the process of sharing Google Sheets files and managing access levels—Viewer, Commenter, and Editor (0:40–4:30).

A significant portion of the video is dedicated to the Protect Sheets and Ranges feature (4:35–10:30). The instructor demonstrates how to:

item.title: Working with Multiple Sheets
item.description:
This video demonstrates how to add, rename, and delete sheets, and how to move and copy data between sheets.

It covers essential skills for organizing and manipulating data across different tabs, including:

  • Managing Sheets: How to add (including the Shift + F11 shortcut), rename, and delete worksheets (0:30 - 1:34, 3:47 - 4:11).
  • Organizing Layout: Techniques for moving and rearranging the order of sheets by dragging or using the ‘Move or Copy’ dialog (1:37 - 2:42).
  • Duplication: How to create exact copies of sheets, which is useful for backups or maintaining consistent formatting (2:43 - 3:46).
  • Data Transfer: Methods for cutting, copying, and pasting data between different sheets, as well as using the undo function (4:19 - 5:10).

Assignment: Exercise 2: Working with Multiple Sheets

item.title: Copying and Pasting
item.description:
This video shows how to copy and paste data within a worksheet, between worksheets, and between workbooks, including Paste Special options.

While it introduces standard copy-paste shortcuts (Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V), it focuses primarily on the advanced Paste Special options available to users (1:16).

Key features covered include:

item.title: Module 4 - 4 - Advanced Question Types in Google Forms
item.description:
This video provides a tutorial on using three advanced question types to improve form design:

  • Multiple Choice Grid (0:43 - 3:13): Explains how to compress multiple questions sharing the same response options into a single, compact grid format.
  • Tick Box Grid (3:13 - 4:21): Demonstrates how to use this for ranking or preference questions where users can select multiple options, including settings to limit responses per column.
  • Linear Scale (7:28 - 8:36): Shows how to create rating-style questions, such as satisfaction surveys, using a customizable numerical scale (e.g., 1-5 or 1-10).

item.title: PivotTables
item.description:
This video explains how to create PivotTables, modify the layout and format, and apply filters to analyse large amounts of data.

Key highlights include:

  • Introduction to Pivot Tables: Explains how to select data and insert a Pivot Table into a new worksheet (2:03 - 2:45).
  • Building Tables: Demonstrates how to use the PivotTable Fields pane to drag and drop fields into Rows, Columns, and Values (3:28 - 4:15).
  • Advanced Analysis: Shows how to perform more complex summaries, such as breaking down data by categories (e.g., blocks and management types) and creating multi-layered reports (5:00 - 9:55).
  • Best Practices: Emphasizes that successful pivot table creation relies on having a clear mental picture of the desired output and encourages users to practice with their own institutional data (9:55 - 11:41).

Assignment: Exercise 4: Pivot Tables

item.title: Formatting Spreadsheets
item.description:
This video focuses on techniques for formatting spreadsheets to make data more presentable and easier to analyze, using cell borders, colours, and styles.

Key topics covered include:

  • Clearing and Applying Styles: How to remove existing formatting and apply new colors and borders (0:47-2:25).
  • Number Formatting: Adjusting settings to display data in specific currency formats (e.g., Rupees) and managing decimal places (2:29-4:52).
  • Conditional Formatting: Using rules to automatically highlight data based on criteria, such as identifying employees who have exceeded a specific number of leave days (4:53-7:00).

Assignment: Exercise 4: Formatting Spreadsheets

item.title: Module 4 - 5 - Responses, Quizzes and Settings
item.description:
This video shows how to view responses in a Google Form. It also shows how to create a quiz using a Google Form and covers other settings.

The video provides as a comprehensive guide to managing Google Forms once they have been distributed. Key topics covered include:

item.title: VLOOKUP
item.description:
This video teaches how to use VLOOKUP to find data from one table and insert it into another table based on a matching value.

Key takeaways include:

  • Understanding VLOOKUP: Explanation of why it is called “vertical” lookup and how it searches columns to match data (0:00-1:34).
  • Step-by-Step Implementation: A demonstration of how to define the lookup value, table array, column index number, and the range lookup (exact vs. approximate match) to pull specific information like enrollment numbers or facility details (1:34-5:10).
  • Best Practices: Tips on using unique identifiers (like U-DISE codes) for accurate matching (5:12-6:26).
  • Limitations: A brief explanation of when to use more advanced tools like INDEX and MATCH functions, specifically when the required data is located to the left of the lookup column (7:34-8:20).

Assignment: Exercise 5: VLOOKUP

item.title: Formatting Data as a Table
item.description:
This video demonstrates how to format data as a table, choose a table style, apply filters, and add a total row.

This video provides a practical guide on formatting data as a table. It explains how this feature enhances data management by enabling easier sorting, filtering, and automated calculations (1:39 - 6:35).

item.title: INDEX and MATCH
item.description:
This video, the final installment in the Advanced Excel Functions course, provides a practical guide on using the INDEX and MATCH functions as a flexible alternative to VLOOKUP (0:00 - 0:49).

Key takeaways include:

  • Understanding the functions: The video explains how MATCH returns the row number of a specific lookup value (1:28 - 2:48), while INDEX retrieves a value from a specified row (2:51 - 3:45).
  • Combining functions: Learn how to nest MATCH inside INDEX to perform lookups regardless of whether the target data is to the left or right of your lookup value (3:52 - 5:27).
  • Practical applications: The instructor demonstrates how to apply these functions to retrieve data like school names, U-DISE codes, and block names across different datasets (5:29 - 8:04).

By the end of the video (8:05 - 9:24), viewers gain a solid understanding of how these functions offer greater versatility than standard lookup tools, allowing for more complex data management in Excel.

item.title: Sorting, Filtering and Data Validation
item.description:
This video shows how to sort data by one or more columns, filter data based on criteria, and use data validation to restrict data entry.

This video provides a practical guide on organizing and protecting data in spreadsheets:

  • Sorting and Filtering (0:01 - 2:48): The video demonstrates how to sort data by numbers, text, or color, and how to use filters to manage both tables and standard cell ranges efficiently.
  • Data Validation (2:52 - 6:22): The tutorial explains how to use data validation to control user input—such as restricting cells to specific numeric ranges (e.g., 0-31 days) or enforcing minimum text character lengths—to ensure data integrity and prevent entry errors.

Assignment: Exercise 6: Sorting, Filtering and Data Validation

item.title: Using Basic Formulas and Functions
item.description: This video serves as an introductory guide to using basic mathematical functions and formulas in Excel. It covers how to structure formulas correctly by starting with an equals sign (=) and demonstrates how to utilize key functions such as SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MAX, and MIN (0:01-1:05).

item.title: Manipulating Text Using Basic Formulas
item.description:
The video provides a practical guide on manipulating text data using various built-in Excel formulas like AND, CONCAT, SPLIT, TRIM, and UPPER.. It demonstrates how to clean, organize, and format text strings effectively within a dataset.

Key topics covered include:

  • Combining Text: Using CONCATENATE and the & (ampersand) operator to join text from different cells (1:18 - 4:43).
  • Splitting Data: Utilizing the Text to Columns feature to separate strings based on delimiters like spaces or hyphens (5:48 - 8:01).
  • Cleaning Data: Applying the TRIM function to remove unnecessary extra spaces from text strings (8:45 - 9:28).
  • Formatting Case: Changing text capitalization using UPPER, LOWER, and PROPER functions (10:00 - 10:59).
  • Advanced Text Manipulation: Measuring string length with LEN and locating/replacing specific characters using FIND and REPLACE functions (11:04 - 14:41).

Assignments:

item.title: Good Practices
item.description:
In this video, the participants will learn the importance of using clear and concise labels, avoiding merged cells, using consistent formatting, and backing up their work regularly to prevent loss of data. They will also learn how to create custom lists.

The video covers essential best practices to help users work more efficiently and professionally in Excel.

item.title: Advanced Excel Functions Assessment
item.description:
1. What does Conditional Formatting in Excel help you achieve? a. Create drop-down lists b. Analyze data with PivotTables X. Highlight important data in spreadsheets d. Apply VLOOKUP to lookup values

  2. How can you count the number of cells in a range that meet a specific condition in Excel? a. Using the SUM function b. Using the CONCATENATE function X. Using the COUNTIF function d. Using the IFERROR function

  3. What is the purpose of using IF statements in Excel? a. To merge data from different tables X. To create conditions that determine the outcome of a cell c. To create PivotTables d. To validate data entry in cells

item.title: Data Analysis with Google Sheets Assessment
item.description:
1. What is the primary use of Google Sheets? a. To create presentations b. To design graphics X. To create and manage spreadsheets d. To edit videos

  2. How can you quickly fill a series of cells with the same data in Google Sheets? X. By using the drag handle (fill handle) from the corner of the cell b. By copying and pasting each cell c. By typing in each cell individually d. By pressing the Enter key repeatedly

item.title: Data Collection with Google Forms Assessment
item.description:
1. Which of the following is the primary purpose of Google Forms? a. To edit images b. To create presentations X. To collect and analyse data through surveys or quizzes d. To manage spreadsheets

  2. What type of question in Google Forms allows respondents to select multiple answers? a. Short answer X. Checkboxes c. Paragraph d. Linear scale

  3. How can you add a new question to a Google Form? X. By clicking the plus (+) button on the right side of the form b. By pressing the Enter key c. By selecting File > New Question d. By right-clicking on the form background

item.title: Data Visualisation Assessment
item.description:
1. What is the primary purpose of data visualisation? a. To add more complexity to data X. To simplify complex data for easier understanding c. To hide important information d. To make data confusing

  2. Which of the below statements indicates the importance of data visualisation? a. It enables us to see patterns and trends that might not be apparent from looking at the raw data b. It makes data analysis more complicated and time consuming X. It enables us to make data-driven decisions based on facts rather than opinions or assumptions d. It is only useful for experts in data analysis

item.title: Introduction to Excel Assessment
item.description:
1. Which of the following mathematical functions in Excel calculates the total of a range of cells? a. AVERAGE b. COUNT X. SUM d. MAX/MIN

  2. How can you adjust the width of a column in Excel? Select all that are correct. X. Right-click on the column header and select “Format Column Width” X. Use the “Adjust Column Width” button on the Home tab X. Drag the boundary between two column headers to resize it d. Use the “Row Height” option in the Format Cells dialog box

item.title: Advanced Excel Functions Assignment
item.description:
Download the worksheet and demo sheet to get started:

  • Advanced Excel Course — Work Sheet
  • Advanced Excel Course — Demo Sheet

Exercise 1: Data Validation 🔗

To be completed after Video 1

  1. Download the file: Advanced Excel Course — Work Sheet

  2. Open the file and go to the sheet called Data Validation.

  3. For the District column (without the header row), add a data validation (list type) to allow only the following value: Kalimpong.

item.title: Data Analysis with Google Sheets Assignment
item.description:
School Data with Google Sheets 🔗

In this assignment, you will work with Google Sheets to analyse school-level data. You will create a new Google Sheet, enter or import some basic data from the UDISE+ database for your district, use a pivot table for analysis, apply conditional formatting, and share the file with a colleague.

Step 1: Gather Data from the UDISE+ Database 🔗

  • Access the UDISE+ database for your district: UDISE+ Report Dashboard
  • Download or note down basic school-level data such as school name, total number of students, number of teachers, Pupil Teacher Ratio (PTR), and infrastructure details.

Step 2: Create a New Google Sheet 🔗

  • Open Google Sheets by going to sheets.google.com.
  • Click on the + Blank option to create a new sheet.
  • Name your sheet, such as “School Data Analysis — [Your District Name]”.

Step 3: Enter or Import the Data 🔗

  • If you have the data in a file like Excel or CSV, import it by clicking on File > Import and selecting your file.
  • If you do not have a file, manually enter the data into your Google Sheet.
  • Ensure your data is neatly organised in rows and columns.

Step 4: Create a Pivot Table to Analyse the Data 🔗

  • Highlight the data you entered, then click on Data > Pivot table.
  • In the Pivot Table editor, choose how you want to analyse the data.
  • Experiment with different arrangements to gain insights from your data.

Step 5: Apply Conditional Formatting 🔗

  • Highlight a column or range of data, such as Pupil Teacher Ratio.
  • Click on Format > Conditional formatting.
  • Set a rule to highlight cells based on their value.
  • Apply the formatting and see how it visually categorises your data.

Step 6: Collaborate by Sharing the Google Sheet 🔗

  • Click on the Share button at the top right of the screen.
  • Enter the email address of a colleague to share the sheet with them.
  • Set the appropriate permissions and send the invitation.

item.title: Data Collection with Google Forms Assignment
item.description:
Creating and Sharing a Google Form 🔗

In this assignment, you will create a Google Form to collect basic personal information from your colleagues. This will help you practice using different question types, customising the form’s appearance, and analysing responses.

Step 1: Create a New Google Form 🔗

  • Open Google Forms by going to forms.google.com.
  • Click on the + Blank option to create a new form.

Step 2: Name Your Form and Add a Description 🔗

  • At the top of the form, give your form a title, such as “Office Personnel Information”.
  • In the description box below the title, briefly explain the purpose of the form, for example: “Please provide your basic information for our office records.”

Step 3: Add Questions to Your Form 🔗

  • Name: Add a Short answer question type asking for the respondent’s name.
  • Qualifications: Add a Multiple choice or Checkboxes question type to allow respondents to select their qualifications, such as BA, BSc, MA etc.
  • Background: Use a Paragraph question type to let respondents write about their professional background.
  • Interests: Use a Checkboxes question type to allow respondents to select their areas of interest and hobbies.
  • Contact Information: Add a Short answer question type for their email address and phone number.
  • You can add more questions as per your requirement.

Step 4: Customise the Form’s Appearance 🔗

  • Click on the Palette icon at the top right of the screen to change the form’s theme.
  • Choose a colour scheme or image that aligns with your office’s branding or personal preferences.
  • Experiment with different fonts and background colours to make your form visually appealing.

Step 5: Add an Image or Video (Optional) 🔗

  • To add an image, click the Image icon in any question or section header and upload an image.
  • To add a video, click the Video icon and search for a YouTube video or paste a video link.

Step 6: Explore Advanced Question Types (Optional) 🔗

  • Experiment with advanced question types like Linear scale, Date, or File upload.

Step 7: Set Up Form Settings 🔗

  • Click on the Settings gear icon at the top of the form.
  • Under the Responses section, choose whether to collect email addresses, allow editing after submission, or restrict responses to one per person.

Step 8: Share the Form with Colleagues 🔗

  • Click on the Send button at the top right of the screen.
  • Choose the Link option to get a shareable link.
  • Copy the link and share it with your colleagues via email or any communication platform.

Step 9: Collect and Explore Responses 🔗

  • Wait for your colleagues to fill out the form.
  • Check the Responses tab to view and analyse the collected data.
  • Explore the summary of responses, individual responses, and the option to export data to Google Sheets for further analysis.

item.title: Data Visualisation Assignment
item.description:
Creating Charts from Real Data 🔗

In this assignment, you will download real school infrastructure data from the UDISE+ portal, summarise it, and create a pie chart in Excel.

  1. Go to the UDISE+ Report Dashboard.

  2. Find and click on the report “Number of Schools by Availability of Infrastructure and Facilities, School Management and School Category” (report ID: 3013).

  3. Select latest year, West Bengal state, and District-wise in the district option.

  4. Download the data by clicking on the green Excel icon on the top right of the report card.

item.title: Introduction to Excel Assignment
item.description:
Exercise 1: Creating a Spreadsheet 🔗

To be completed after Video 2

  1. Create a new Excel file and save it on your device with the file name Excel Practice.

  2. Enter the following data in your Excel sheet:

S. No. Name State Designation Remarks Salary Sick Leaves
1 John Delhi Manager John is a very hardworking fellow 30,000 2
2 Maria Goa Head Clerk 25,000 3
3 Sukumar West Bengal Peon 15,000 0
4 Shivam Uttar Pradesh Manager 30,000 5

  1. Ensure the following:

item.title: Excel Formulas Reference Sheet
item.description:
Arithmetic Formulas 🔗

  • Addition: =A1 + B1
  • Subtraction: =A1 - B1
  • Multiplication: =A1 * B1
  • Division: =A1 / B1
  • ROUND: =ROUND(A1, 2) (Rounds the value in cell A1 to 2 decimal places)

Common Functions 🔗

  • SUM: =SUM(A1:A5) (Adds the values in cells A1 to A5)
  • AVERAGE: =AVERAGE(A1:A5) (Calculates the average of values in cells A1 to A5)
  • COUNT: =COUNT(A1:A5) (Counts the number of cells with values in the range A1 to A5)
  • MAX: =MAX(A1:A5) (Returns the maximum value in the range A1 to A5)
  • MIN: =MIN(A1:A5) (Returns the minimum value in the range A1 to A5)

Text Functions 🔗

  • CONCATENATE: =CONCATENATE(A1, " ", B1) (Joins the text in cells A1 and B1 with a space in between)
  • UPPER: =UPPER(A1) (Converts the text in cell A1 to uppercase)
  • LOWER: =LOWER(A1) (Converts the text in cell A1 to lowercase)
  • PROPER: =PROPER(A1) (Capitalizes the first letter of each word in the text in cell A1)
  • TRIM: =TRIM(A1) (Removes extra spaces from the text in cell A1)
  • SUBSTITUTE: =SUBSTITUTE(A1, "old", "new") (Replaces occurrences of “old” with “new” in the text in cell A1)
  • LEN: =LEN(A1) (Returns the number of characters in the text in cell A1)
  • LEFT: =LEFT(A1, 3) (Returns the leftmost 3 characters from the text in cell A1)
  • RIGHT: =RIGHT(A1, 3) (Returns the rightmost 3 characters from the text in cell A1)
  • MID: =MID(A1, 2, 5) (Returns a substring from the text in cell A1, starting from the 2nd character and extracting 5 characters)

Logical Formulas 🔗

  • IF: =IF(A1 > B1, "Yes", "No") (Checks if A1 is greater than B1 and returns “Yes” if true, “No” if false)
  • AND: =AND(A1 > 5, B1 < 10) (Checks if both A1 is greater than 5 and B1 is less than 10)
  • OR: =OR(A1 > 5, B1 < 10) (Checks if either A1 is greater than 5 or B1 is less than 10)
  • NOT: =NOT(A1 > 5) (Checks if A1 is not greater than 5)
  • COUNTIF: =COUNTIF(A1:A5, ">10") (Counts the number of cells in the range A1 to A5 that are greater than 10)
  • SUMIF: =SUMIF(A1:A5, ">10", B1:B5) (Adds the corresponding values in the range B1 to B5 for cells in the range A1 to A5 that are greater than 10)
  • AVERAGEIF: =AVERAGEIF(A1:A5, ">10", B1:B5) (Calculates the average of values in the range B1 to B5 if the corresponding value in the range A1 to A5 is greater than 10)
  • IFBLANK: =IF(A1="", "Blank", "Not Blank") (Checks if cell A1 is blank and returns “Blank” if true, “Not Blank” if false)

Date and Time Functions 🔗

  • TODAY: =TODAY() (Returns the current date)
  • NOW: =NOW() (Returns the current date and time)
  • DATEDIF: =DATEDIF(A1, B1, "d") (Calculates the number of days between dates in cells A1 and B1)

Lookup and Reference Functions 🔗

  • VLOOKUP: =VLOOKUP(A1, A2:B10, 2, FALSE) (Searches for a value in column A and returns the corresponding value from column B)
  • HLOOKUP: =HLOOKUP(A1, A2:F5, 3, FALSE) (Searches for a value in row 1 and returns the corresponding value from row 3)
  • INDEX: =INDEX(A1:A10, 3) (Returns the value at the third position in the range A1 to A10)
  • MATCH: =MATCH(A1, A1:A10, 0) (Finds the position of a value in the range A1 to A10)

Sort Data 🔗

  1. Filter: Use the filter function to display only specific data based on criteria.
